title: Coordinate Axes
description: Robot draws X and Y axes with labels, then marks the origin and a few sample points.

steps.label: Draw X axis
steps.prompt: draw a horizontal line from left to right across the center of the paper with a small arrowhead at the right end and the letter X near it
steps.tutor_narration: This horizontal line is the X axis. Every point to the right has a positive X value, and every point to the left has a negative X value.

steps.label: Draw Y axis
steps.prompt: draw a vertical line from bottom to top through the center of the paper with an arrowhead at the top and the letter Y near it
steps.tutor_narration: And this vertical line is the Y axis. Up is positive Y, down is negative Y. Together, these two axes create a coordinate grid.

steps.label: Mark origin
steps.prompt: draw a small circle at the exact center of the paper where the two axes cross, with the label 0,0 next to it
steps.tutor_narration: The point where the axes cross is called the origin — that is position (0, 0). Every other position on the paper is measured from here.

steps.label: Mark sample points
steps.prompt: draw three small dots on the paper at different positions and write coordinate labels next to each one like (100,50) (200,150) and (50,180)
steps.tutor_narration: These dots show three different positions. Each one has an X coordinate and a Y coordinate. The robot knows exactly where to go because of these numbers.

title: Bezier vs Linear Paths
description: Robot draws the same curve two ways — jagged straight segments versus a smooth Bezier — to show the difference.

steps.label: Jagged zigzag path
steps.prompt: draw a path across the paper that zigzags sharply between 6 points in a rough wave shape with very angular corners
steps.tutor_narration: This is what happens when a robot just connects points with straight lines. Notice how sharp and rough the turns are? Real machines don't like moving that way — it's jerky and slow.

steps.label: Smooth Bezier curve
steps.prompt: draw a smooth flowing S-curve across the paper that gently curves from left to right passing through the same general area as the zigzag above
steps.tutor_narration: Now watch this. The robot draws the exact same path, but using a Bezier curve. See how it flows smoothly? This is how race cars take corners, how airplanes turn, and how drawing robots draw.

title: Circle from Polygons
description: Robot draws a triangle, then a hexagon, then a 12-sided shape, then a circle — showing how polygons approach a circle.

steps.label: Triangle (3 sides)
steps.prompt: draw a large equilateral triangle centered in the middle of the paper
steps.tutor_narration: Three sides. Very obvious corners. This is as far from a circle as you can get.

steps.label: Hexagon (6 sides)
steps.prompt: draw a large regular hexagon centered in the middle of the paper at the same size as the triangle
steps.tutor_narration: Six sides. The corners are softer now. You can start to see the circle hiding inside.

steps.label: 12-sided polygon
steps.prompt: draw a large regular 12-sided polygon centered in the middle of the paper
steps.tutor_narration: Twelve sides. Now it really starts to look round. Where are the corners?

steps.label: 60-sided polygon (circle)
steps.prompt: draw a large smooth circle centered in the middle of the paper
steps.tutor_narration: Sixty sides. At this point, you can not see the corners at all — it looks exactly like a circle. That is literally what a circle IS in the robot's math: a polygon with so many sides the corners disappear.

title: Sine Wave
description: Robot draws a sine wave across the paper while the tutor explains frequency and amplitude.

steps.label: Single sine wave
steps.prompt: draw one complete smooth sine wave across the full width of the paper with 2 complete cycles
steps.tutor_narration: This is a sine wave. The robot calculates each Y position using the formula Y equals sine of T. As T increases — that is, as we move right — the sine function smoothly goes up and down.

steps.label: Double frequency wave
steps.prompt: draw a sine wave across the paper with 4 complete cycles at the same amplitude as the first wave
steps.tutor_narration: I doubled the frequency. Now there are twice as many peaks in the same space. This is exactly how musical notes work — higher frequency means higher pitch. The robot is literally drawing sound.

title: PID Control Demo
description: Robot draws a wobbly overcorrecting line first, then a perfectly straight line to show good vs bad control.

steps.label: Bad control (overshoot)
steps.prompt: draw a horizontal line across the paper that oscillates wildly above and below the intended straight path like a snake gradually settling down
steps.tutor_narration: This is what happens with bad control tuning. The robot is trying to go straight, but it overcorrects — when it is too far up, it jerks down too hard, then overshoots the other way. This is called oscillation.

steps.label: Good control (tuned PID)
steps.prompt: draw a perfectly straight horizontal line across the paper
steps.tutor_narration: With properly tuned PID control, the robot draws a perfectly straight line. It still corrects itself constantly — but the corrections are exactly the right size. Proportional, Integral, Derivative: three numbers that make this possible.

title: Reflection Symmetry
description: Robot draws a shape, pauses, then draws its mirror image to demonstrate bilateral symmetry.

steps.label: Draw vertical axis
steps.prompt: draw a light vertical dashed line down the center of the paper
steps.tutor_narration: This is the axis of symmetry — the mirror line. Everything on the left will be reflected perfectly to the right.

steps.label: Draw left half
steps.prompt: draw half of a butterfly wing shape on the left side of the center vertical line
steps.tutor_narration: Here is the left half of our shape. Now watch — the robot will calculate the exact reflection of every point across that center line.

steps.label: Draw reflected right half
steps.prompt: draw the mirror image of a butterfly wing on the right side of the center line to complete the butterfly shape
steps.tutor_narration: The right half is the mathematical reflection of the left. For every point at X on the left, the mirror point is at negative X on the right. This is how architects design buildings, how engineers design jet engines, and how nature builds butterflies.

title: AprilTag Localization
description: Robot demonstrates how it finds the paper using AprilTag markers at the corners.

steps.label: Mark tag positions
steps.prompt: draw four small squares at the four corners of the paper to represent AprilTag markers
steps.tutor_narration: These four squares represent the AprilTag markers printed on your paper. The robot camera can recognize these unique patterns from any angle.

steps.label: Draw canvas boundary
steps.prompt: draw a rectangle connecting the four corner markers to show the detected canvas boundary
steps.tutor_narration: Once the robot sees all four tags, it calculates the exact shape and position of the paper in 3D space. This is called homography — mapping between the camera image and the real world.

steps.label: Draw calibration cross
steps.prompt: draw a small cross at the exact center of the paper and a dot at each corner
steps.tutor_narration: Now the robot can place any drawing precisely on the paper — no matter where the paper is, what angle it is tilted, or how far away the camera is. This is the same technology used in augmented reality apps and self-driving cars.
